analysis.analysis_was_performed_on: Decorative panels of the Japanese Tower, Royal Castle Domain, Laeken, Brussels, Belgium

object_area_sample.object_area_sample_label: Paint micro-sample C90.058 / P210.011 — arsenic sulfide mixed with ultramarine blue, protein-based medium, underlayer

study_object_sample.taken_from: Decorative panels of the Japanese Tower, Royal Castle Domain, Laeken, Brussels, Belgium

sample_preparation.preparation_notes: The bulk sample was fixed to a 1 cm3 poly(methyl methacrylate) cube using white PVA glue, after which acrylic copolymer resin (Spofacryl, Spofa Dental CZ-10031, Prague) was poured on the sample and a second cube placed on top. The cross-section was left to dry, then polished with water until nearly exposed, and subsequently polished using Micro Mesh polishing cloths in a sequence of grits (2400, 4000, 6000 and 8000 mesh) to a mirror-like surface. The cross-section was gold-coated for SEM-EDX analysis. For PLM, a fraction of the remaining loose sample was dispersed and mounted in Canada balsam (n = 1.55).
sample_preparation.used_method: Mechanical cross-section preparation (acrylic resin embedding and polishing); gold sputter-coating for SEM-EDX; Canada balsam mounting for PLM

object_area_sample.object_area_sample_label: Paint micro-sample C90.094 / P209.066 — arsenic sulfide mixed with Prussian blue, urushi-containing oil medium, finishing layer

study_object_sample.taken_from: Decorative panels of the Japanese Tower, Royal Castle Domain, Laeken, Brussels, Belgium

sample_preparation.preparation_notes: The bulk sample was fixed to a 1 cm3 poly(methyl methacrylate) cube using white PVA glue, after which acrylic copolymer resin (Spofacryl, Spofa Dental CZ-10031, Prague) was poured on the sample and a second cube placed on top. The cross-section was left to dry, then polished with water until nearly exposed, and subsequently polished using Micro Mesh polishing cloths in a sequence of grits (2400, 4000, 6000 and 8000 mesh) to a mirror-like surface. The cross-section was gold-coated for SEM-EDX analysis. For PLM, a fraction of the remaining loose sample was dispersed and mounted in Canada balsam (n = 1.55).
sample_preparation.used_method: Mechanical cross-section preparation (acrylic resin embedding and polishing); gold sputter-coating for SEM-EDX; Canada balsam mounting for PLM

object_area_sample.object_area_sample_label: Paint micro-sample C90.101 / P209.074 — unmixed arsenic sulfide, urushi-containing finishing layer

study_object_sample.taken_from: Decorative panels of the Japanese Tower, Royal Castle Domain, Laeken, Brussels, Belgium

sample_preparation.preparation_notes: The bulk sample was fixed to a 1 cm3 poly(methyl methacrylate) cube using white PVA glue, after which acrylic copolymer resin (Spofacryl, Spofa Dental CZ-10031, Prague) was poured on the sample and a second cube placed on top. The cross-section was left to dry, then polished with water until nearly exposed, and subsequently polished using Micro Mesh polishing cloths in a sequence of grits (2400, 4000, 6000 and 8000 mesh) to a mirror-like surface. The cross-section was gold-coated for SEM-EDX analysis. For PLM, a fraction of the remaining loose sample was dispersed and mounted in Canada balsam (n = 1.55).
sample_preparation.used_method: Mechanical cross-section preparation (acrylic resin embedding and polishing); gold sputter-coating for SEM-EDX; Canada balsam mounting for PLM

analysis.analysis_type: Multi-technique (SEM-EDX, Micro-Raman Spectroscopy, Polarized Light Microscopy)
analysis.analysis_title: Identification of artificial orpiment in the interior decorations of the Japanese Tower in Laeken, Brussels, Belgium
analysis.notes: Multi-technique analytical study aimed at identifying the arsenic sulfide pigment used in the decorative panels of the Japanese Tower at the Royal Castle Domain in Laeken, Brussels, Belgium. The study was motivated by the unusually good conservation state of the arsenic sulfide pigment despite its known tendency to fade upon light exposure. The pigment was found applied with different painting techniques: bound with oil and urushi in lacquer layers, and with an aqueous (protein-based) binder in mat relief panels, where it is always applied as an underlayer mixed with ultramarine blue. Among 165 samples taken from the tower for a complete technical study, 24 samples containing arsenic sulfide pigment sampled on different floors were selected. Three representative cross-sections were discussed in detail: C90.058/P210.011, C90.094/P209.066, and C90.101/P209.074. The arsenic sulfide was identified as amorphous arsenic sulfide glass (g-AsxSx), synthesized from arsenic trioxide and sulfur via a dry process. The presence of As4S4 monomer units at different ratios across samples suggests manufacture in different batches and at different quenching temperatures. The unpublished full technical report is archived at KIK-IRPA under number 2010.10826.

place.place_name: Laboratory of polychrome artifacts, Royal Institute for Cultural Heritage (KIK-IRPA), Parc du Cinquantenaire 1, B-1000 Brussels, Belgium

agent.institution: Laboratory of polychrome artifacts, Royal Institute for Cultural Heritage, Parc du Cinquantenaire 1, B-1000 Brussels, Belgium
Laboratory of polychrome artifacts, Royal Institute for Cultural Heritage, Parc du Cinquantenaire 1, B-1000 Brussels, Belgium
AXES Research Group, Department of Chemistry, University of Antwerp, Groenenborgerlaan 171, 2020 Antwerp, Belgium

agent.n: AXES Research Group, University of Antwerp

method.method_name: Scanning Electron Microscopy with Energy Dispersive X-ray Detection (SEM-EDX)
method.method_notes: Backscattered electron imaging, elemental mapping and semi-quantitative point analyses on gold-coated cross-sections to identify elemental composition and morphology of pigment particles.
Raman spectroscopy used to identify the type of arsenic sulfide present based on wavenumber profile and band ratios, and to detect sulfur remainders from dry-process synthesis.

Investigation of particle shapes, interference colors and optical properties (isotropy/anisotropy) in Canada balsam mounts under slightly polarized and cross-polarized light, to distinguish arsenic sulfide glass from natural orpiment and to infer starting materials.

device.device_name: Renishaw inVia multiple laser dispersive Raman spectrometer
Zeiss Axio Imager 2 optical microscope (PLM and photomicrography)
JEOL Cross Section Polisher IB-09010CP (reference sample preparation only)
SPI-MODULE Sputter Coater (gold coating of cross-sections)

component.component_name: Peltier-cooled near-infrared enhanced deep-depletion CCD detector

software.software_notes: Version 4.06, Oxford Instruments. Used for EDX data collection and semi-quantitative elemental analysis on SEM cross-sections.
Renishaw Wire 2 software used for Raman spectra acquisition. Spectra were subsequently baseline corrected when necessary.

setting_values.setting_value: Raman power on sample: 1 mW (reduced with neutral density filters)
Optical microscope UV excitation bandpass filter: 390-420 nm
Cross Section Polisher accelerating voltage (reference samples only): 4.0 kV
Cross Section Polisher argon gas flow (reference samples only): 4.4
Cross Section Polisher duration (reference samples only): 12 hours

dataset.dataset_title: Natural orpiment reference material — Kremer Pigmente GmbH & Co, Aichstetten, Germany
Laboratory-prepared arsenic sulfide glass (g-AsxSx) — synthesized from arsenic trioxide and sulfur (1:1), Bunsen burner method after Rotter
Identification of artificial orpiment in the interior decorations of the Japanese tower in Laeken, Brussels, Belgium
Interdisciplinary technical study of the Japanese Tower at the Royal Castle Domain in Laeken (unpublished report, KIK-IRPA archive no. 2010.10826)
